column1: Buddha's Life and Teachings: Comprehensive Timeline

column2: Synopsis
column4: Details

column2: Born as Prince Siddhartha to King Suddhodana and Queen Maya of the Sakya clan. His birth was accompanied by various miracles and prophecies about his future as either a great king or spiritual leader.
column4: The birth included Queen Maya giving birth while standing and holding onto a sal tree branch. Multiple deities attended the birth, and the infant supposedly took seven steps, declaring this would be his final rebirth.

column2: Raised in royal luxury with his father attempting to shield him from all suffering, hoping he would become a great ruler rather than a spiritual teacher.
column4: Educated in all royal arts and sciences. Excelled particularly in archery and other martial skills. Married to Princess Yasodhara at age 16.

column2: On four separate excursions outside the palace, Siddhartha encountered an old person, a sick person, a corpse, and a wandering ascetic. These sights revealed to him the reality of suffering and the possibility of spiritual liberation.
column4: The four sights were supposedly arranged by divine beings to ensure his spiritual awakening. After seeing the ascetic, he became determined to seek enlightenment.

column2: At age 29, shortly after the birth of his son Rahula, Siddhartha left the palace at night to pursue the spiritual path.
column4: Cut his hair as a symbol of renunciation. His horse Kanthaka was said to have died of heartbreak after carrying him away from the palace.

column2: Studied under two meditation teachers (Alara Kalama and Uddaka Ramaputta) before embarking on six years of extreme ascetic practices, nearly starving himself to death.
column4: Mastered the meditative attainments under his teachers but found they did not lead to complete liberation. His ascetic practices were so extreme that he became emaciated and nearly died.

column2: Realizing extreme asceticism was not the path to enlightenment, he accepted rice milk from a village girl named Sujata and adopted the "Middle Way" between indulgence and self-mortification.
column4: Sujata thought he was a tree deity and offered him milk-rice in a golden bowl. His five ascetic companions abandoned him, believing he had given up the spiritual quest.

column2: Seated under the Bodhi tree, he vowed not to rise until achieving enlightenment. After defeating Mara (the embodiment of temptation and distraction), he attained complete awakening at dawn.
column4: The enlightenment process involved several watches of the night: first recalling past lives, then understanding karma, and finally realizing the Four Noble Truths and dependent origination.

column2: Spent seven weeks in various locations near the Bodhi tree, contemplating his realization and deciding whether to teach others.
column4: Different traditions describe these weeks differently, but they typically include the Buddha experiencing the bliss of liberation, being sheltered by a naga king during a storm, and being offered food by merchants.

column2: Delivered the "Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta" (Setting the Wheel of Dharma in Motion) to his five former companions, who became his first disciples.
column4: Taught the Four Noble Truths and the Noble Eightfold Path. All five ascetics attained stream-entry during this discourse.

column2: Gradually gathered more disciples, including wealthy patrons like Anathapindika and former nobility like Yasa.
column4: Early conversions include the three Kassapa brothers and their 1,000 fire-worshipping disciples, and the king's chief ministers Sariputta and Moggallana.

column2: Returned to his homeland and reconciled with his family. His son Rahula, father King Suddhodana, cousin Ananda, and many Sakyan nobles joined the Sangha.
column4: The Buddha performed miracles to impress his proud relatives. Established rules about parental consent after his father complained about Rahula's ordination.

column2: Initially reluctant, the Buddha eventually agreed to establish an order of nuns (Bhikkhuni Sangha) after his stepmother Mahapajapati and other women shaved their heads and walked to find him.
column4: The Buddha's attendant Ananda advocated on behalf of the women. The Buddha established additional rules for the nuns' order.

column2: The Buddha's cousin Devadatta attempted to take over the Sangha, tried to kill the Buddha multiple times, and created a schism in the community.
column4: Devadatta gained supernatural powers through meditation but was consumed by ambition. His attempts included rolling a boulder at the Buddha and setting a drunken elephant loose to charge him.

column2: For 45 years after enlightenment, the Buddha traveled throughout the Ganges plain, teaching various audiences and establishing the community of practitioners.
column3: Numerous suttas throughout the Pali Canon
column4: Established monastic retreats during the rainy season. Primary teaching locations included Savatthi, Rajagaha, and Vesali.

column2: At age 80, the Buddha accepted his final meal from the blacksmith Cunda, fell ill, and gave his final instructions to his disciples before passing away (parinirvana) in Kusinara.
column4: Refused to appoint a successor, telling disciples to rely on his teachings. His final words were "All conditioned things are subject to decay. Strive on with diligence."

column2: Synopsis
column4: Details

column2: The Buddha's central teaching: 1) The truth of suffering (dukkha), 2) The cause of suffering (tanha/craving), 3) The cessation of suffering (nirodha), 4) The path leading to cessation (magga).
column4: Often described as similar to a medical diagnosis: identifying the ailment, the cause, the prognosis, and the treatment. Each truth has three aspects (knowledge, practice, and realization).

column2: The Buddha's practical guide to liberation: Right View, Right Intention, Right Speech, Right Action, Right Livelihood, Right Effort, Right Mindfulness, and Right Concentration.
column4: Organized into three categories: wisdom (right view, intention), ethical conduct (right speech, action, livelihood), and mental discipline (right effort, mindfulness, concentration).

column2: A twelve-link chain explaining how ignorance leads to suffering and rebirth, and how understanding this process leads to liberation.
column4: The twelve links are: ignorance, formations, consciousness, name-and-form, six sense bases, contact, feeling, craving, clinging, becoming, birth, and aging-and-death.

column2: The teaching that there is no permanent, unchanging self or soul.
column4: Taught that all phenomena are "not-self" and that clinging to anything as "self" leads to suffering. This discourse led to the enlightenment of the first five disciples.

column2: The teaching that all conditioned phenomena are transient and subject to change.
column4: Nothing that comes into existence remains unchanged; everything is in constant flux. Recognition of this truth leads to dispassion.

column2: A discourse on liberation through detachment from the six senses and their objects.
column4: Delivered to 1,000 former fire-worshipping ascetics. The Buddha described the senses and their objects as "burning" with the fires of passion, aversion, and delusion.

column2: Instructions on developing universal, boundless good will toward all beings.
column4: One of the four "divine abodes" (brahmaviharas) along with compassion, sympathetic joy, and equanimity. Described as the most effective protection.

column2: Systematic instructions on establishing awareness of body, feelings, mind states, and mental objects.
column4: Often considered the most detailed meditation instructions given by the Buddha. Presents mindfulness as the direct path to purification and liberation.

column2: The Buddha's instructions to his young son on ethical conduct and meditation.
column4: Used the metaphor of a mirror for self-reflection and emphasized truthfulness. Later taught him mindfulness of breathing.

column2: Guidance for householders on ethical living, wealth management, and spiritual practice within family life.
column4: Covered topics like the duties within relationships, avoiding evil deeds, proper use of wealth, and balancing worldly concerns with spiritual practice.

column2: Encouragement not to rely on tradition, authority, or speculation but to test teachings through direct experience.
column4: Often cited as an example of the Buddha's emphasis on critical thinking. Encourages evaluating teachings based on whether they lead to harm or benefit when put into practice.

column2: A parable explaining that the Dharma (teaching) is like a raft for crossing over, not for carrying around; even good teachings should eventually be released.
column4: Illustrates that attachment even to correct teachings can become an obstacle; the purpose of the teaching is to reach liberation, not to become a new source of clinging.

column2: Illustrates the urgency of spiritual practice by comparing metaphysical questions to a man refusing treatment for a poisoned arrow until he knows all details about the archer.
column4: The Buddha refused to answer certain metaphysical questions as they were not conducive to liberation. He emphasized focusing on the Four Noble Truths instead.

column2: Various similes illustrating the transient nature of life, including comparing life to dewdrops, bubbles, lightning, and drawing lines on water.
column4: These metaphors emphasize that life is precious and brief, encouraging urgency in spiritual practice.

column2: Synopsis
column4: Virtue/Lesson

column2: As Prince Vessantara, the Bodhisattva practiced perfect generosity, giving away his magical rain-bringing elephant, his wealth, his kingdom, and even his children and wife before all was restored to him.
column4: Perfect generosity (dana paramita). Considered the most important Jātaka in Theravada Buddhism; often recited during festivals.

column2: As a rabbit, the Bodhisattva offered himself as food by jumping into a fire when he had nothing else to give a hungry beggar (who was actually Sakka, king of the gods, testing him).
column4: Self-sacrifice and generosity. The rabbit's image was placed on the moon as a reminder of this deed.

column2: As King Sivi, the Bodhisattva gave his eyes to a blind Brahmin (actually Sakka in disguise). His sight was later restored due to the power of his truthful determination.
column4: Generosity and determination. Shows willingness to sacrifice parts of one's body for others' benefit.

column2: As the Patience Ascetic, the Bodhisattva was tortured by a drunk king but maintained perfect equanimity and compassion, even toward his torturer.
column4: Patience (khanti) and non-anger even under extreme provocation.

column2: As Prince Mahajanaka, the Bodhisattva persevered swimming in the ocean for seven days after a shipwreck when all others gave up. Later, as king, he renounced his throne for spiritual life.
column4: Perseverance (viriya) and renunciation. Shows the importance of effort even when success seems impossible.

column2: As the youth Sama/Suvannasama, the Bodhisattva cared for his blind parents after they retired to the forest. Shot by a king while gathering water, he was revived through the power of his parents' truthful declaration about his virtue.
column4: Filial piety and dedication to family duties. Illustrates the power of truth and virtue.

column2: As Prince Temiya, the Bodhisattva pretended to be deaf, mute, and crippled for 16 years to avoid becoming king and potentially creating negative karma. Eventually escaped to become an ascetic.
column4: Determination and renunciation. Shows willingness to endure hardship to avoid harming others.

column2: As King Sutasoma, the Bodhisattva was captured by the man-eating King Porisada but was temporarily released to keep a promise to hear teachings from a Brahmin. He returned as promised and converted Porisada.
column4: Truthfulness and the power of keeping one's word. Shows how wisdom and integrity can transform even the most evil person.

column2: As King Nimi, the Bodhisattva was so virtuous that the gods invited him to visit heaven. During the journey, he witnessed various heavenly rewards and hellish punishments, reinforcing his commitment to virtue.
column4: Ethical conduct and the consequences of actions. Explores karma and its results.

column2: As the wise minister Vidhura, the Bodhisattva was won in a dice game and claimed by a naga (serpent) queen who wanted his heart. Through his wisdom, he convinced all parties of his value alive rather than dead.
column4: Wisdom (pañña) and skillful speech. Shows how intelligence can overcome dangerous situations.

column2: As a monkey king, the Bodhisattva used his body as a bridge to save his troop from danger. Though exhausted, he saved everyone but was killed by a treacherous human he had also rescued.
column4: Self-sacrifice and leadership responsibility. A powerful example of putting others before oneself.

column2: As the leader of golden geese, the Bodhisattva was captured in a trap. His loyal captain refused to abandon him, demonstrating the power of friendship and loyalty.
column4: Friendship (mittata) and loyalty. Illustrates the bond between true friends in adversity.

column2: As the leader of golden geese, the Bodhisattva was caught in a trap set by a greedy king but used wisdom and compassion to gain freedom for himself and his flock.
column4: Compassion and wisdom. Shows how to respond skillfully to those who harm us.

column2: As the brilliant youth Mahosadha, the Bodhisattva used extraordinary intelligence to solve complex problems, navigate court intrigues, and prevent a destructive war.
column4: Wisdom and skillful means. One of the longest Jātakas, full of clever solutions to difficult problems.

column2: The Bodhisattva appeared as Prince Canda, who opposed a corrupt minister's advice that human sacrifice would grant the king entry to heaven. Though initially unsuccessful, truth eventually prevailed.
column4: Standing up against wrong views and unethical practices, even at personal risk.

column2: As a wise advisor named Takkāriya, the Bodhisattva used skillful means to reveal the corruption of four other advisors who were misleading the king.
column4: Honesty and ethical conduct in leadership. Shows the importance of surrounding oneself with virtuous advisors.

column2: As the king of Kuru, the Bodhisattva and his kingdom practiced five precepts so perfectly that their virtue brought rain during a drought in another kingdom.
column4: Ethical conduct (sila) and its power to benefit many beings. Demonstrates how virtue can have far-reaching effects.

column2: As a naga prince named Bhuridatta, the Bodhisattva observed the Uposatha precepts despite capture and brutal treatment by a snake charmer.
column4: Ethical discipline and determination. Shows commitment to spiritual practice despite extreme hardship.

column2: As the naga king Campeyya, the Bodhisattva endured torture from a snake charmer while maintaining his commitment to the precepts.
column4: Patience and adherence to principles even under duress. Similar themes to the Bhuridatta Jātaka.

column2: As Prince Mahajanaka, the Bodhisattva persevered swimming in the ocean for seven days after a shipwreck when all others gave up. Later, as king, he renounced his throne for spiritual life.
column4: Perseverance (viriya) and renunciation. Shows the importance of effort even when success seems impossible.

column1: 4. Other Important Buddhist Narratives and Texts

column2: Synopsis
column4: Significance

column2: Chronicles the lineage of 28 Buddhas who preceded Gautama Buddha, including their lives, characteristics, and key disciples.
column4: Establishes Gautama Buddha within a spiritual lineage and cosmic timeframe. Shows how the path to Buddhahood develops over many lifetimes.

column2: Collection of 35 stories about the Buddha's previous lives, organized specifically to illustrate the perfections (paramis/paramitas).
column4: Demonstrates how the Bodhisattva methodically developed the qualities needed for Buddhahood across lifetimes.

column2: Collection of biographical stories about the Buddha and his disciples, explaining their current circumstances as results of past actions.
column4: Popular in Sanskrit Buddhist traditions. Shows how karma connects past actions to present circumstances.

column2: A dialogue between the Indo-Greek King Menander (Milinda) and the Buddhist monk Nagasena, exploring complex philosophical questions.
column4: Famous for its analogies explaining difficult concepts like non-self. Shows Buddhism engaging with Hellenistic culture.

column2: The story of a wealthy layman who outshines many disciples with his profound understanding of emptiness and skillful teaching methods.
column4: Particularly important in Mahayana Buddhism. Challenges hierarchy between monastic and lay practitioners.

column2: Contains parables and stories revealing the Buddha's skillful means in teaching different audiences according to their capacity.
column4: Central text in many Mahayana traditions. Famous parables include the Burning House and the Prodigal Son.

column2: Chronicles how Emperor Asoka converted to Buddhism after the horrors of the Kalinga War and spread the Dharma through non-violence.
column4: Shows Buddhism's transition from a local movement to an imperial religion. Important for understanding Buddhism's early spread.

column2: Traces the lineage from Bodhidharma to Hui-neng, including the famous story of Hui-neng's awakening and succession after writing a verse.
column3: Platform Sutra of the Sixth Patriarch
column4: Central to Zen/Chan Buddhism's self-understanding. Emphasizes direct insight over intellectual understanding.

column2: Biography of the Tibetan yogi who overcame a violent past through intense practice under Marpa to become one of Tibet's most beloved saints.
column4: Shows the possibility of transformation from great evil to great holiness within one lifetime. Key text in Tibetan Buddhism.

column2: Accounts of the tantric master who established Buddhism in Tibet, including his magical contests with local deities and spirits.
column4: Central to the Nyingma school of Tibetan Buddhism. Shows Buddhism adapting to and transforming local religious landscapes.

column1: 5. Chronological Timeline of Buddhist Textual Development

column2: Key Developments
column4: Historical Context

column2: The Buddha's lifetime and oral transmission of teachings
column3: Original discourses (later recorded in various canons)
column4: Period of religious innovation in North India, also saw the rise of Jainism and other śramaṇa movements

column2: First Buddhist Council and continuing oral transmission
column3: Early organization of teachings into nikayas/agamas
column4: Period after the Buddha's passing, with the Sangha maintaining discipline and teachings

column2: Asoka's reign and spread of Buddhism
column3: Asokan Edicts, early versions of canonical texts
column4: Mauryan Empire unified much of South Asia; Asoka's patronage spread Buddhism widely

column2: Sectarian developments, beginning of written texts
column3: Early versions of Vinaya and Sutta Pitakas, Abhidharma texts
column4: Buddhism divided into various schools (traditionally 18); early Mahayana texts begin to emerge

column2: First written versions of Pali Canon
column4: Political instability in North India; Sri Lanka becomes a center for Theravada Buddhism

column2: Development of Mahayana literature
column3: Early Perfection of Wisdom texts, Lotus Sutra, Pure Land sutras
column4: Kushan Empire in North India; Buddhism spreads along Silk Road to Central Asia and China

column2: Flowering of Buddhist philosophy and tantric developments
column3: Madhyamaka and Yogacara texts, early tantras
column4: Gupta period in India; Buddhism flourishes in university centers like Nalanda

column2: Mature development of tantric Buddhism
column3: Completion of tantric systems in India
column4: Period of integration in Indian Buddhism; challenged by revival of Hinduism and later Islamic invasions

column2: Decline in India, flourishing in Tibet & East Asia
column4: Buddhism largely disappears from India; becomes fully established in Tibet, China, Japan, and Korea

column4: Cultural Adaptations

column4: Integrated with existing Brahmanical and indigenous traditions

column2: Theravada Pali Canon and commentarial literature
column3: Preservation of early Buddhist texts and monastic discipline
column4: Strong emphasis on Jātaka tales in art and popular practice

column2: Mixture of early sectarian texts and Mahayana sutras
column3: Important transmission zone between India and China
column4: Buddhist texts translated into local languages like Khotanese and Sogdian

column2: Chinese Agamas, Mahayana sutras, indigenous compositions
column4: Adaptation to Confucian and Daoist concepts; development of uniquely Chinese schools

column2: Kangyur (Buddha's words) and Tengyur (commentaries)
column3: Comprehensive collection of late Indian Buddhism
column4: Integration with Bon practices; emphasis on tantric lineages and guru devotion

column2: Chinese-derived canon plus indigenous compositions
column3: Selective emphasis on particular sutras by different schools
column4: Development of distinctly Japanese forms like Pure Land Buddhism and Zen

column2: Chinese-derived canon with local commentaries
column4: Integration with local religious practices and social structures

column3: Parables from the Lotus Sutra; Buddha's diverse teaching methods
